summary: Policy-backed control for picture password sign-in for domain users. Turning it off removes one sign-in option for domain accounts, but it can simplify credential policy and support expectations.

setting.name: Picture Password Sign-In for Domain Users
setting.casual_explanation: Picture password lets someone sign in by tapping or drawing gestures on an image. This policy controls whether domain users are allowed to use that sign-in method.
setting.why_people_change_it: Organizations disable it when they want fewer sign-in methods for domain accounts or want a more traditional credential baseline. They leave it available when domain users still want that sign-in convenience.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it off removes a gesture-based sign-in method for domain users, but it can make sign-in rules more consistent in managed environments.
setting.professional_notes: CredentialProviders.admx documents BlockDomainPicturePassword under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. The local ADML help text says enabling the policy blocks domain users from setting up or signing in with a picture password.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Domain users can use the normal Windows behavior for picture password sign-in.

allowed_values.label: Picture password allowed
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and domain users can set up and use picture password sign-in.

allowed_values.label: Picture password blocked
allowed_values.meaning: Domain users cannot set up or sign in with a picture password.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes BlockDomainPicturePassword = 1, which matches the documented enabled policy state that turns off picture password sign-in for domain users.

windows_defaults.label: Windows policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows systems supporting the BlockDomainPicturePassword policy

states.rationale: If the policy is not configured, domain users can use picture password sign-in.

windows_defaults.notes: This record is specifically about domain-user behavior, not local non-domain account behavior.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Environments that still allow picture password sign-in
avoid_for: Organizations that want to reduce alternate sign-in methods
recommended_profiles.good_when: You still want domain users to be able to choose picture password sign-in.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want a stricter or more uniform domain sign-in baseline.
tradeoffs: Picture password remains available to domain users.
There is one more sign-in method to support and govern.

recommended_profiles.label: Block picture password
avoid_for: Users or environments that still rely on picture password convenience

states.rationale: Prevent domain users from setting up or using picture passwords.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want domain accounts to use more conventional sign-in flows only.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You intentionally support picture password for domain users.
tradeoffs: Domain-user picture password sign-in is removed.
Credential policy is simpler for managed environments.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft CredentialProviders.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps BlockDomainPicturePassword to HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft CredentialProviders.adml help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text says enabling the policy prevents domain users from setting up or signing in with a picture password, while disabling or not configuring it allows picture password use.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes BlockDomainPicturePassword = 1 for the current tweak.

decision.why: The local Microsoft Credential Providers policy files explicitly define the policy path HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System, the value name BlockDomainPicturePassword, and the enabled/disabled mapping. The app writes the documented enabled-state value 1 on that exact policy surface.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="BlockDomainPicturePassword" class="Machine" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System" valueName="BlockDomainPicturePassword"> <enabledValue><decimal value="1" /></enabledValue> <disabledValue><decimal value="0" /></disabledValue>; CredentialProviders.adml: "If you enable this policy setting, a domain user can't set up or sign in with a picture password."
validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft ADMX file defines the exact policy path and value name used by the app, with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. The paired ADML help text explains that enabling the policy blocks picture password sign-in for domain users.
